sections.title: 🐳 Docker Quick Review
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
CONTAINER = your app + its dependencies, packaged together
→ Same container runs identically: developer laptop, CI/CD, production
→ Isolated from other containers on same machine
→ Lightweight (shares OS kernel — starts in seconds, not minutes like VMs)

DOCKERFILE = instructions to build your container image
FROM node:18-alpine              # Start from base image
WORKDIR /app                     # Set working directory
COPY package*.json ./            # Copy package files
RUN npm install --production     # Install dependencies (layer cached)
COPY . .                         # Copy your source code
EXPOSE 3000                      # Document which port app uses
CMD ["node", "server.js"]        # Command to start app

DOCKER IMAGE = the built artifact (read-only template)
DOCKER CONTAINER = running instance of an image

IMAGE LAYERS:
→ Each instruction in Dockerfile = new layer
→ Layers are CACHED: if layer hasn't changed, Docker reuses cached layer
→ OPTIMIZE: put things that change least (dependencies) early,
             things that change most (your code) last
→ Bad:  COPY . .  then  RUN npm install  (code change = reinstall everything)
→ Good: COPY package*.json . → RUN npm install → COPY . .
        (code change only invalidates last COPY layer)
```

---

revisionPoints: → Same container runs identically: developer laptop, CI/CD, production
→ Isolated from other containers on same machine
→ Lightweight (shares OS kernel — starts in seconds, not minutes like VMs)
→ Each instruction in Dockerfile = new layer
→ Layers are CACHED: if layer hasn't changed, Docker reuses cached layer
→ OPTIMIZE: put things that change least (dependencies) early,
→ Bad:  COPY . .  then  RUN npm install  (code change = reinstall everything)
→ Good: COPY package*.json . → RUN npm install → COPY . .

sections.title: 📦 ECR — Elastic Container Registry
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
ECR = private Docker image registry on AWS

WHY ECR OVER DOCKER HUB:
→ Private by default (Docker Hub public by default)
→ IAM-controlled access (no separate login management)
→ Same region as ECS → fast pulls → fast container startup
→ No rate limiting (Docker Hub limits free pulls to 100/6hours)
→ Vulnerability scanning built-in (scans on push or on demand)
→ Image lifecycle policies (auto-delete old images to save cost)

PUSHING IMAGE TO ECR:
# Authenticate Docker to ECR
aws ecr get-login-password --region us-east-1 | \
  docker login --username AWS \
  --password-stdin 123456789.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com

# Build image
docker build -t myapp:v1.2.3 .

# Tag for ECR
docker tag myapp:v1.2.3 \
  123456789.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/myapp:v1.2.3

# Push to ECR
docker push 123456789.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/myapp:v1.2.3

ECR LIFECYCLE POLICIES:
→ "Keep only last 10 images tagged with 'release'"
→ "Delete untagged images older than 7 days"
→ Prevents storage costs from accumulating (every CI/CD push = new image)
→ $0.10/GB/month for storage

IMAGE SCANNING:
→ Enhanced scanning: powered by Amazon Inspector
→ Scans: OS packages, language packages (npm, pip, gem)
→ Reports: critical, high, medium, low severity CVEs
→ Integrate with Security Hub: centralized finding management
→ Scan on push (recommended) or on demand
```

---

sections.title: 🚢 ECS — Elastic Container Service
sections.rawMarkdown:
### Task Definition — The Blueprint

```
TASK DEFINITION = JSON config describing how to run your container
Think: docker-compose.yml but for AWS

KEY SETTINGS IN TASK DEFINITION:
family:        "myapp-api"          # Name for this task definition
cpu:           256                   # Total CPU units (256 = 0.25 vCPU)
memory:        512                   # Total memory in MB
networkMode:   "awsvpc"             # Each task gets own ENI (required for Fargate)
executionRole: "ecsTaskExecutionRole" # Pull from ECR, write logs to CloudWatch
taskRole:      "myapp-task-role"    # What your APP can access (S3, DynamoDB, etc.)

containers:
  - name:   "myapp"
    image:  "123456789.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/myapp:v1.2.3"
    cpu:    256
    memory: 512
    portMappings:
      - containerPort: 3000
    environment:
      - name: NODE_ENV
        value: production
    secrets:                         # From Secrets Manager or Parameter Store
      - name: DB_PASSWORD
        valueFrom: "arn:aws:secretsmanager:..."
    logConfiguration:
      logDriver: awslogs
      options:
        awslogs-group: /ecs/myapp
        awslogs-region: us-east-1

TWO IAM ROLES IN TASK DEFINITION:
executionRole: ECS agent uses this to:
  → Pull image from ECR
  → Write container logs to CloudWatch
  → Read secrets from Secrets Manager / SSM Parameter Store

taskRole: Your CONTAINER code uses this to:
  → Call S3, DynamoDB, SQS, etc.
  → This is the "IAM Role for EC2" equivalent for containers
  → Give it least-privilege permissions for what your app needs
```

### ECS Launch Types

```
EC2 LAUNCH TYPE:
→ You manage a fleet of EC2 instances (the ECS cluster)
→ ECS agent runs on each EC2
→ Tasks placed ON your EC2 instances
→ You handle: EC2 patching, scaling, right-sizing, AZ distribution
→ More control, more complexity
→ Use when: need specific hardware (GPU), or cost optimize at scale

FARGATE LAUNCH TYPE (recommended):
→ No EC2 instances to manage — AWS manages the underlying compute
→ You define: image + CPU + memory + IAM role
→ AWS handles: where to run it, patching, scaling infrastructure
→ Pay per second of task runtime (vCPU + memory)
→ Use for: most workloads, teams that want less operational overhead
→ Slightly more expensive than EC2 per unit but zero ops cost

FARGATE PRICING:
vCPU: $0.04048/vCPU/hour
Memory: $0.004445/GB/hour

Example: 1 vCPU, 2 GB, running 24/7:
($0.04048 + 2 × $0.004445) × 24 × 30 = $36.50/month

vs On-Demand t3.medium (2 vCPU, 4GB): $29.95/month
→ Fargate slightly pricier but zero server management cost
```

### ECS Service — Long-Running Apps

```
SERVICE = ECS component that maintains N running tasks
→ Defines: task definition, desired count, deployment strategy
→ If task crashes → Service restarts it automatically
→ Integrates with ALB for HTTP traffic distribution
→ Supports rolling updates and blue-green deployments

ECS SERVICE SETTINGS:
desiredCount: 3            # Always keep 3 tasks running
minimumHealthyPercent: 50  # During deployment: at least 50% must be healthy
maximumPercent: 200        # During deployment: can have 200% (double) temporarily

DEPLOYMENT STRATEGIES:
Rolling Update (default):
→ Stops some tasks, starts new version tasks
→ minimumHealthyPercent/maximumPercent control how many at once
→ No additional infrastructure cost
→ Rollback: redeploy previous task definition version

Blue/Green (with CodeDeploy):
→ Green = new version running alongside Blue (old version)
→ ALB has: production listener (Blue) + test listener (Green)
→ Shift traffic: immediate or canary (10% → 50% → 100%)
→ Rollback: shift traffic back to Blue (seconds)
→ More controlled, safer, but needs CodeDeploy setup

SERVICE AUTO SCALING:
→ Scale ECS service tasks based on CloudWatch metrics
→ Target Tracking: "keep average CPU at 50%"
→ Step Scaling: "if CPU > 80% → add 2 tasks"
→ Scheduled: "every weekday 8am → set desired to 10"
→ Integrates with ALB for request-count-based scaling
```

### ECS vs EKS

```
ECS (Elastic Container Service):
→ AWS-proprietary container orchestration
→ Simpler: less configuration, AWS-native integration
→ Deep integration: ALB, IAM, CloudWatch, CodeDeploy
→ Fargate support: excellent
→ Learning curve: gentle (if you know AWS, easy to learn ECS)
→ Vendor lock-in: ECS is AWS-only
→ Use when: new to containers, AWS-only, want simplicity

EKS (Elastic Kubernetes Service):
→ Managed Kubernetes (open-source, industry standard)
→ More complex: Kubernetes is feature-rich but complex
→ Portable: same Kubernetes config works on any cloud (GKE, AKS)
→ Ecosystem: huge community, Helm charts, operators, tons of tooling
→ Learning curve: steep (Kubernetes is complex)
→ Cost: $0.10/hour for EKS control plane (~$72/month)
→ Use when: team already knows K8s, need portability, complex workloads,
  want to avoid vendor lock-in

REALITY CHECK:
→ Most companies building new on AWS → start with ECS (simpler, faster)
→ Teams with existing K8s expertise → EKS (leverage existing knowledge)
→ Large enterprises → EKS (portability, K8s ecosystem)
→ "Should I learn K8s?" — Yes, it's the industry standard
   "Should I use EKS for my new startup?" — Probably ECS first

EKS PRICING:
$0.10/hour control plane + EC2/Fargate for worker nodes
ECS: FREE control plane + EC2/Fargate for tasks
→ EKS costs $72/month MORE than ECS just for control plane
```

---

→ Green = new version running alongside Blue (old version)

ECS (Elastic Container Service):

→ Simpler: less configuration, AWS-native integration
→ Deep integration: ALB, IAM, CloudWatch, CodeDeploy
→ Fargate support: excellent
→ Learning curve: gentle (if you know AWS, easy to learn ECS)
→ Vendor lock-in: ECS is AWS-only
→ Use when: new to containers, AWS-only, want simplicity

EKS (Elastic Kubernetes Service):
→ Managed Kubernetes (open-source, industry standard)
→ More complex: Kubernetes is feature-rich but complex
→ Portable: same Kubernetes config works on any cloud (GKE, AKS)
→ Ecosystem: huge community, Helm charts, operators, tons of tooling
→ Learning curve: steep (Kubernetes is complex)
→ Cost: $0.10/hour for EKS control plane (~$72/month)
→ Use when: team already knows K8s, need portability, complex workloads,

REALITY CHECK:
→ Most companies building new on AWS → start with ECS (simpler, faster)
→ Teams with existing K8s expertise → EKS (leverage existing knowledge)

   "Should I use EKS for my new startup?" — Probably ECS first

EKS PRICING:
$0.10/hour control plane + EC2/Fargate for worker nodes
ECS: FREE control plane + EC2/Fargate for tasks
→ EKS costs $72/month MORE than ECS just for control plane

→ ECS agent runs on each EC2
→ Tasks placed ON your EC2 instances
→ You handle: EC2 patching, scaling, right-sizing, AZ distribution

→ No EC2 instances to manage — AWS manages the underlying compute

sections.title: 🎯 Interview Q&A — Containers
sections.rawMarkdown:
**Q: Lambda vs ECS Fargate — how do you decide?**
```
"Primary question: is the workload event-driven or always-on?

Lambda wins for event-driven, short-duration work:
Triggered by S3 upload, SQS message, HTTP request, schedule.
Runs for milliseconds to minutes. Scales to zero (pay nothing when idle).
No infrastructure to manage. Perfect for: file processing, webhooks,
scheduled jobs, API backends with variable traffic.
Limitation: max 15 minutes, cold starts can add latency.

ECS Fargate wins for always-on services:
Long-running processes, WebSocket connections, background workers consuming
queues continuously, services that benefit from persistent connections,
tasks > 15 minutes (video transcoding, ML inference serving).
Cost: you pay for the full container runtime whether request is happening or not.
No cold starts. Predictable latency.

The practical rule: if it's triggered by events and runs for seconds/minutes
→ Lambda. If it needs to run continuously or > 15 minutes → ECS Fargate.

Many architectures use both: ECS for the main API (always-on, low latency),
Lambda for async processing (image resize on upload, email sending, etc.)."
```

**Q: How do you manage secrets in ECS containers?**
```
"Never put secrets in environment variables if they contain sensitive values,
because they're visible in the ECS console, CloudWatch logs, and metadata service.

Correct approach: Secrets Manager or SSM Parameter Store.

In Task Definition, use the secrets field (not environment):
{
  'name': 'DB_PASSWORD',
  'valueFrom': 'arn:aws:secretsmanager:us-east-1:123456:secret:prod/db-password'
}

ECS agent fetches the secret at task startup and injects it as an environment
variable inside the container. The secret value never appears in the task
definition. The ECS task execution role needs permission to read from Secrets Manager.

For rotation: Secrets Manager can auto-rotate (Lambda-based rotation).
When rotated, ECS tasks read the new value on next restart. For immediate
rotation: force new deployment of the ECS service (new tasks read new secret).

For configuration (non-sensitive): SSM Parameter Store Standard tier is free.
For sensitive secrets needing rotation: Secrets Manager ($0.40/secret/month)."
```

**Q: ECS tasks keep restarting in production. What is your debugging approach?**
```
"I split it into image startup, health checks, and resource pressure.

First check ECS service events:
→ task failed to start?
→ task killed due to failing ALB health checks?
→ essential container exited?

Then CloudWatch logs:
→ did app crash on boot?
→ missing env var or secret?
→ migration script blocking startup?

Then resource signals:
→ OutOfMemory in container logs
→ CPU pegged at 100%
→ container memory setting too low

Then health check alignment:
→ ALB health check path correct?
→ startup time longer than grace period?
→ app listening on correct container port?

One very common production issue is this:
the app is healthy after 90 seconds, but ALB starts checking at 15 seconds,
marks it unhealthy, ECS replaces it, and the service flaps forever.
Senior fix: increase grace period, add startup/readiness behavior, and separate
'process is alive' from 'service is ready for traffic'."
```

**Q: ECS or EKS for a new platform team? How do you answer without being dogmatic?**
```
"I would answer based on org constraints, not tool fashion.

Choose ECS when:
→ team is AWS-centric
→ speed and simplicity matter more than portability
→ platform team is small
→ workloads are straightforward APIs, workers, cron jobs

Choose EKS when:
→ company already has Kubernetes expertise
→ you need ecosystem tools that assume Kubernetes
→ multi-cloud portability is a real business requirement
→ platform engineering maturity is already high

The mistake is choosing EKS because it is 'more advanced'.
Kubernetes gives flexibility, but it also gives you more surface area for outages:
ingress, CNI, autoscalers, controllers, admission webhooks, node lifecycle.

For many teams, ECS gets them to a stable production platform faster.
The senior answer is choosing the simplest platform that your team can operate well at 2 a.m."
```

---

First check ECS service events:
→ task failed to start?
→ task killed due to failing ALB health checks?
→ essential container exited?

Then CloudWatch logs:
→ did app crash on boot?
→ missing env var or secret?
→ migration script blocking startup?

Then resource signals:
→ OutOfMemory in container logs
→ CPU pegged at 100%
→ container memory setting too low

Then health check alignment:
→ ALB health check path correct?
→ startup time longer than grace period?
→ app listening on correct container port?

One very common production issue is this:
the app is healthy after 90 seconds, but ALB starts checking at 15 seconds,
marks it unhealthy, ECS replaces it, and the service flaps forever.
Senior fix: increase grace period, add startup/readiness behavior, and separate
'process is alive' from 'service is ready for traffic'."

qaItems.question: ECS or EKS for a new platform team? How do you answer without being dogmatic?
qaItems.answer:
"I would answer based on org constraints, not tool fashion.

Choose ECS when:
→ team is AWS-centric
→ speed and simplicity matter more than portability
→ platform team is small
→ workloads are straightforward APIs, workers, cron jobs

Choose EKS when:
→ company already has Kubernetes expertise
→ you need ecosystem tools that assume Kubernetes
→ multi-cloud portability is a real business requirement
→ platform engineering maturity is already high

The mistake is choosing EKS because it is 'more advanced'.
Kubernetes gives flexibility, but it also gives you more surface area for outages:
ingress, CNI, autoscalers, controllers, admission webhooks, node lifecycle.

For many teams, ECS gets them to a stable production platform faster.
The senior answer is choosing the simplest platform that your team can operate well at 2 a.m."

revisionPoints: → Lambda. If it needs to run continuously or > 15 minutes → ECS Fargate.
→ task failed to start?
→ task killed due to failing ALB health checks?
→ essential container exited?
→ did app crash on boot?
→ missing env var or secret?
→ migration script blocking startup?
→ OutOfMemory in container logs

sections.title: 🔗 Connects To
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
Lambda ← S3/SQS/SNS/EventBridge/API Gateway/ALB: triggers
Lambda → DynamoDB/S3/RDS/SQS/SNS: what it calls
Lambda → VPC: optional, for private resource access
Lambda → RDS Proxy: for database connection pooling
Lambda → CloudWatch: logs + metrics + X-Ray traces
Lambda → Layers: shared code/libraries

ECR ← CI/CD pipeline: pushes images on deploy
ECR → ECS: ECS pulls images to run tasks
ECR → Lambda: container image Lambda functions
ECR → Inspector: vulnerability scanning

ECS ← ECR: pulls container images
ECS → ALB: service registers tasks as targets
ECS → CloudWatch: logs from containers (awslogs driver)
ECS → Secrets Manager/SSM: secrets injection at startup
ECS → Service Auto Scaling: scale tasks based on metrics
ECS ← CodeDeploy: blue/green deployments

NEXT: API Gateway — REST and WebSocket APIs
The managed front door for your serverless and container backends.
```

---
---
